claims.text: Iran has enacted legislation imposing transit fees on oil and gas carriers transiting the Strait of Hormuz, establishing a durable leverage mechanism over energy shipping routes that European nations depend on for renewable infrastructure buildout.
Hormuz closure fears have directly delayed large offshore wind component shipments to EU and UK projects, as corroborated by The Guardian reporting in March 2026.

European nations are leading a diplomatic push to reopen the Strait of Hormuz specifically as an energy security concern, confirming that renewable infrastructure supply chains are now embedded in a contested maritime theatre.
CSIS and ODNI assessments confirm Iran's demonstrated intent and capability to use energy infrastructure targeting as an instrument of geopolitical coercion.

MuddyWater, aligned to Iran's Ministry of Intelligence and Security, has confirmed pre-positioned persistent access via Dindoor and Fakeset backdoors inside critical infrastructure supply chain firms, airports, and financial institutions, as reported in the CISA joint advisory of June 2025.

CyberAv3ngers, operating under IRGC Cyber Electronic Command, has developed and deployed IOCONTROL malware targeting internet-exposed industrial control and OT devices, with Censys identifying 40,000–48,000 such exposed devices on U.S. networks constituting an active attack surface.

Iran's confirmed OT pre-positioning in analogous critical infrastructure sectors — aligned with its escalating coercive posture — creates a plausible and assessed pathway for targeted system intrusion against energy operators with Mediterranean supply chain exposure.

No confirmed incident of OT-to-cloud lateral movement or wind turbine IP exfiltration — including blade design data or predictive maintenance algorithms — has been identified in the current collection window. This gap limits confidence in assessing whether the intrusion pathway has been activated against wind sector targets specifically.

AeroGrid's Mediterranean operations — Palermo manufacturing (HIGH criticality) and Malaga service hub — sit within the disrupted maritime theatre, with the North African logistics corridor directly exposed to Hormuz-linked supply delays affecting component delivery timelines.

The system intrusion scenario ranks #3 globally by financial impact; the MED VaCR stands at $4,200,000 against the Predictive Maintenance and Offshore IP Theft scenario, with offshore blade assembly process and Mediterranean service network identified as the primary crown jewels at risk.
